what_it_does: Acceptance test for the filesystem-backed store that verifies the three hot durable write paths (event-log append, ref pointer update, graph save) call os.fsync and round-trip data intact, and that read paths call no fsync.
observations: Wraps os.fsync with a counter to assert the durability syscall contract.
Tests append_event expects >=1 fsync and data round-trips via events_since.
Tests set_ref with put_content expects >=2 fsync (file + directory) and round-trips via head/get_content.
Tests save_graph expects >=2 fsync (file + directory) and round-trips via load_graph.
Verifies read paths (head, load_graph, recent_events, get_content) trigger zero fsync calls.
Uses a temporary directory prefixed company-durability- and removes it on exit.
Restores the real os.fsync in finally blocks after each instrumentation phase.
standouts: Treats os.fsync invocation count as the observable proof of the durability contract.
Explicitly checks directory fsync after atomic rename for set_ref and save_graph.
Enforces a write/read asymmetry: durability only on writes, cheap reads without fsync.
conventions: Uses a module-level _check helper to print PASS/FAIL and accumulate results.
Uses sys.path.insert to import the parent package.
Uses tempfile.mkdtemp for an isolated test root and shutil.rmtree for cleanup.
Restores monkey-patched os.fsync in finally blocks.
concerns: Monkey-patching os.fsync globally is fragile if any concurrency or nested instrumentation exists.
Assumes implementation-specific fsync counts (>=1, >=2) rather than a black-box durability behavior.
Does not verify which file descriptors are fsync'd, only the total call count.
The comment about ext4 (/tmp) and avoiding /mnt/c is not enforced by the code.
Hardcoded expectations may break if the store changes its fsync strategy.
notes: Acceptance test that counts os.fsync calls to prove the three hot write paths are durable and read paths stay cheap.
questions: Does FsStore call os.fsync directly, or could it use a wrapper that bypasses the monkey patch?
Are the expected fsync counts a documented public contract or an implementation detail?
How does this test behave on filesystems where directory fsync has different semantics?
Is the temporary directory guaranteed to be on stable storage in all CI environments?
apparent_themes: Atomic rename plus directory fsync for crash safety.
Write/read cost asymmetry in storage design.
intention_signals: To guarantee that acknowledged writes survive power loss by verifying the store explicitly fsyncs both file and directory file descriptors after atomic renames, while keeping read paths lightweight.
novelty: Uses counting of os.fsync invocations as the acceptance signal for durability rather than crash simulation or hardware testing.
contribution: An acceptance test that documents and guards the FsStore durability contract for hot writes and the fsync-free read invariant.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test for a filesystem-backed store that instruments os.fsync to count invocations, verifying that append_event, set_ref, and save_graph flush data and directory entries to disk and round-trip correctly while read paths invoke no fsync.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the store's three durable write paths explicitly fsync to stable storage and that reads remain fsync-free.

symbols.does: Prints PASS/FAIL for a single assertion and returns the boolean result.
symbols.description: Small helper used by main to log each acceptance check; it prints a formatted line with the label and the ok status and returns ok so the overall result can be accumulated.

symbols.does: Runs the durability acceptance checks against a temporary FsStore.
symbols.description: Sets up a temporary store, monkey-patches os.fsync to count calls, verifies that append_event, set_ref, and save_graph invoke fsync and round-trip data, then verifies that read paths invoke no fsync, and exits with 0 or 1.

symbols.does: Increments a call counter and delegates to the real os.fsync.
symbols.description: Local closure installed as os.fsync during instrumentation; it counts each fsync invocation so the test can assert the durability contract was exercised, while still performing the actual flush.
